speaker: interviewer
text: Adelle Chabelski interviewing Stanley Goodrich. And today is March 20, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stanley, for people who don't know the circumstances in Poland, someone might say when you were in the forest working, why didn't you try to run away? Not just you, but you and the others, why didn't you try to run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Like in Rabka, we were working in the forest. We were not guarded. And we could have walked away. That's very true.

speaker: interviewer
text: But where would you walk? We knew from the-- we knew from, from experiences, from during the war, that some people run away into the forest and got killed. In our-- in our part of the country, of the Polish country, the AK used to be quite active. And they, if they found a Jew, not only did-- either they killed them or they just brought them back to the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no place where to run. There was no place where to go. That's why people didn't go. They didn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's hard to understand for people who, who don't know the circumstances.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no place to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so you actually thought it out and made a decision to, to stay, because you realized the surroundings were unfriendly?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Where would you-- where would you go shelter? We can get no shelter. We get no food. Where would we go? At least we were together. We were together with friends. I mean, whatever is going to be will be. We had hope. We had hope. There always-- there always was hope that maybe the war's going to end. Maybe we're  going to survive. Maybe we're going to work hard, we, we won't get killed. We want to survive. We didn't know the, the machinery of the Germans, the killings. We didn't know that. We didn't even know in 1942 that they shipped all the Jews to, to Belzec. There were rumors that they went to, to some camps, they went to Belzec. But we didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the rumors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the rumors were some people were getting killed. There were rumors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know how? How were they getting killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't know. I, I didn't know. We didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when there were all those transports to the east, did you think people were being relocated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. That's what-- that's what-- that's what we thought. Yes. We thought that people were being relocated to the east.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm, mm-hmm. But you heard about Belzec?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew. We heard in Rabka that they were shipped out to Belzec.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what the rumors were. I don't know, some people. found out that they went to Belzec. And that's, that's that's what did happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: The commandant in Rabka, you said you were a, a witness at the trial. Did he exhibit particular cruelty when it came to that hanging, when you described the hanging situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, he was. You know, the people were working to, to be hanged. They were-- we were building a bigger garage, a [GERMAN], they called it, a garage. And they were hanged around, around the corner. And they were just-- he was just going out and hitting the people to, to to step on-- there were little boxes, like wooden boxes-- to step on the boxes. Going around, just hitting the people-- and make it faster. Go up. Get up. Get up. Get faster-- with the [GERMAN], as you know. And this guy got beaten. What I just mentioned to you before, the guy who begged him, kill me. Shoot me. Shoot me, because he, he, he came down three times, three or four times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because he was too heavy for the rope?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the ropes were very, very thin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very thin, you know. They were not ropes. It was pieces of strings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pieces of strings. And then  he just begged him, shoot me, shoot me. And he, he, he just took the [GERMAN] and hit him quite a few times. No, he said, you will hang. You will hang. For nothing. For no-- for, for no crime, for no-- for nothing. For just he wanted to see him hang.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there something in particular with the hangings in these camps? The Germans used those as some kind of-- to, to scare the other prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that, that was the reason, I guess, it was excuse for the-- because the one person ran away from the camp, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which he didn't survive anyway. He never did survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that prevent people also from escaping, because of what would happen to others if they did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure it did, because you knew that you going to escape, they're going to take people that you know, your friends or hostages, and they're going to kill them. It was known. If you, you run away, they're going to take 10 or 20, or whatever. They're going to hang them. They're going to kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you mentioned you went to Plaszow after Rabka. When did you go? When were you sent to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were shipped to Plaszow-- they, they, they took us to Plaszow in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. 66 of you or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. So whoever was left--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. They sent whoever was left.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Rabka, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them went to-- some of them went to Montelupich. There used to be Gestapo in Krakow. They called it a big jail in Montelupich, I guess. And the people who were hand-workers, like carpenters, cabinet makers, tailors, shoe, shoe makers, they were shipped to Montelupich. People who worked on the manual work, they were shipped to, to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till 19-- till January, 1945. We were the last to leave Plaszow by foot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you remember about Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plaszow, I, I remember that killing on the-- on the-- on the-- they call it Hujowa Gorka. They used to--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. What did they call it? [INAUDIBLE] Gorka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hujowa Gorka, they used to call it. It's kind of-- it's a name they used to call it, that particular spot. It used to be a big hill with a big-- with a big-- what do you call it? Big, big holes, you know. Not holes. What do they call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Wells?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. Like, like a [INAUDIBLE]. You know what they call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Ditches?

speaker: interviewee
text: Big, big, big, deep ditches. Right. And they used to bring people from, from Montelupich, [INAUDIBLE] Gestapo, as you know. [INAUDIBLE] Jewish people. We don't know. Jewish and maybe Polish people. They knew there was a lot of Polish people political. And they used to kill them there. They used to take them up from-- I saw it myself. They used to bring them with big trucks. They used to take them out from the trucks to the-- to the-- to the ravine, and just kill them with little machine guns. And also people from the camps, if they were sick or old or did something, they took them over there, and they killed them. They shot them. And in 1944, end of '44, we had to dig all this-- all the-- all the thing out, and we had to burn all the people on, on a-- on a-- on a-- on, on a stakes of wood. We had to dig all these-- all these people out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember why? Did you know why you had to dig them up and burn them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there, there were rumors that the people-- that the Germans didn't want to leave any witnesses. They didn't want to leave anything to, to, to, to see that they killed so many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any evidence, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, evidence. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to dig up all those bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: All those bodies and, and burn them on the stakes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944, the end of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was before liquidation of, of the Plaszow ghetto-- Plaszow concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- this sounds strange, but I don't know how else to ask this. Were you shocked to see so many dead bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. It was-- it was terrible digging out the bodies, pieces of, of, of, of bones, pieces of, of, of clothes, pieces of, of, of teeth. It was-- it was terrible. The stench when they're burning was terrible. And we were afraid we were going to be-- end-- we're going to end up just like them. We were burning day and night.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you cope psychologically? How did you deal with this? What were you thinking? How were you surviving from moment to moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be honest with you, I don't know how we-- how we survived this. It's amazing that what we came-- we came through, what we went through. And we survived. We were sane. And we survived. We could eat and, and, and go out, and go next day to work, it's quite unbelievable that it could happen to human beings and they can survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that having to survive under those conditions affected your life after the war too, the way that you live, the way that you felt things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They affected in a way. You know, you look different at the world today than, than after that experiences. You want-- you don't want to see people getting punished, people getting killed, people-- prejudice. You hate to see that prejudice. You hate to see what did happen. But we were quite-- we were-- we expecting more, that after the war, when we-- when we survived, we thought we were going to be looked at as, as, as, as survivors, as people which survived such a thing. And, and people will think that, that nothing-- that what happened to us will never happen again. They will say, oh my god, you survived. My-- you know. That didn't happen, you know. Their life goes on the same way as it did. And you have the same prejudice. And after the war in Germany, you, you, you see even the American army came. And they-- sometimes they were even prejudiced against the displaced persons, in other words, against the, the survivors, and helping the Germans. There were a lot-- there were some incidents like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember those incidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're saying Americans were more sympathetic sometimes towards the Germans than the displaced people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't say that all Americans were, were, were sympathetic to the Germans. But I mean, they, they-- you, you see a lot of incidents that they, they couldn't look at the-- at the Jews, particularly Jews, the displaced persons. And, and they, they valued more the Germans than they valued the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you explain that psychologically? What is it about-- what was-- what was it about that situation that made them act differently with one group than the other? What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I think, you know, they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the sentiments of, of, of, of America-- I don't know if the-- all the Americans, but American people are still with, with the-- with the Germans, with the Europeans. Even they fought a war against the Germans. I had incidents coming in Germany after the war. And they picked us up. We were on the-- on the railroads, in the railroad station, you know. After the war in Germany, there were a lot of people just, just traveling. They had to travel with, with the railroad. And the biggest-- the biggest congregation of the people were at the-- at the railroad stations, waiting for trains, going places. And me and-- myself and another friend of mine, we were traveling from city to city. We were sitting on our, our baggage. And the MP came through, German MP-- I mean, American MP. And they just looked at us. We were dressed maybe different than the Germans. And they picked us up. And they, they searched us. And they found pictures of-- a friend of mine had pictures from, from concentration camp, from, from crematorium, you know, little tiny pictures. Somebody must have taken pictures in like a crematorium, I don't know, from Dachau or something. They took the pictures from us away. We had some money. They took the money away. And they say, they're fucking Jews. And they gave us back-- and they gave us to the-- to the railroad police, the German railroad police. And we were with them for quite a few hours, and they released us. They walked away, just like that. That was the accident-- the incident that I went through. And I couldn't speak English. And I couldn't defend myself. It was quite an experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that, that the victims, the survivors, especially after the war, were less civilized-- seemed less civilized than, let's say, some of the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they, they-- I think, how could it be that the Germans were civilized after they went-- what they did to the-- to the-- to the people, to not only the Jews?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. But I said seemed, seemed, meaning physically, because they weren't the ones that went through years of, of starvation and deprivation. Could that-- could that have been--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. I, I cannot explain it exactly if it was because of that. Because it's only of, of, of prejudice, of hatred. You know incidents about now. We're talking about the history. You're talking General Patton, we know about that he-- that he not exactly-- he not-- he didn't favor the, the DPs, the Jewish people in, in Germany. That's what the history tells you now. And the Jewish people were-- they were confined to certain places. They couldn't-- to the DP camps. I was not in the DP camps, by the way. But they were confined to the DP camps. They couldn't move from place to place. And they regulated them in a way, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do you remember about Plaszow? Did you ever see commandant Amon Goth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, quite a few times. I seen Amon riding on a horse and walking through and walking through with Chilowicz. Chilowicz was the, the Jewish [GERMAN]. In other words, the Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] commander of the-- of the concentration camp-- him and Finkelstein, and whatever, you know. I didn't have any, any close [INAUDIBLE] with, with, with Goth. I'd just seen him pass by, going by, and you didn't look at him too, too much. You didn't want that they look at you, especially at close range. I've seen when they killed all these-- all these Jewish leaders from the-- from the-- from the-- from Plaszow, like Chilowicz and Finkelstein and his wife, and all, all the-- all these [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] leaders of the camp. As you know, they, they got killed in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all shot. And they were all laid out around the barracks. And everybody had to march through to look at them. And from that day on, when they killed all these Jewish leaders, they made the Germans kapos, the Germans brought  in from Germany. And they made these people leaders of the concentration camp. Mostly were, you know, very few politicals. Mostly were green, with a green [INAUDIBLE] like homosexual or, or, you know I don't know. Either homosexual or some other crimes, you know, murders or something. And they were the leaders. They were the kapos. They were the leaders of our camp. There were some political too, not many.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they treat the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad, you know. They were-- they were a big change. You know, the Jew-- I have to say even the Jewish leader didn't treat us exactly well. Some of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the Germans were, were, you know, walking around with the [GERMAN]. And they were just, you know, very, very-- what they say? Not-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you know, not cruel. Well, how do they say that? Very, very, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] very, very gruesome, you know. Anything, but they would beat you with the [GERMAN]. With the [GERMAN], you'd get 20 on the-- on the-- on the back or 30 on the back, whatever, for little things. Were you there the day when the children were transported to Auschwitz, put on the trucks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, I remember the day well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were all standing on the Appelplatz, which was a big, big hill. It was a-- I think it must have been on Sunday, Sunday afternoon or something. And they were playing music. We had-- we had big loudspeakers on some of them. They were playing German music.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of music? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: German music. I don't know. I don't recall the music.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to remember the music, but it was so many years ago. You're talking about over 50-some years ago, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it unusual for them to have roll call at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was unusual?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. It was not-- it was not a morning roll call. This was an afternoon roll call. And then they picked up all these people. And we could see them. There was a road not too far from the-- from the-- from the Appelplatz. And they were taking all these kids out on these-- on these trucks and, and, and wagons. And you could hear-- you could hear a lot of crying and screaming and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you and the others realize what was happening to the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You know, they shipped them out. But I, I don't know where they shipped them. I think they shipped them to Auschwitz. I'm not sure where. We didn't think too much about it. But we knew what's going on. But you had so much to do with yourself, I guess. You didn't think about it. You didn't-- you didn't-- you didn't sit down and analyze and, and think about it. It was just the daily, daily life going through.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think helped you to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think helped you to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just by luck I survived. My, my youth. I was-- I was-- I was healthy. And I was lucky. I was the right time in the right place, because lots of my friends were just as young as I am, and they did not survive. By-- just by coincidence, they were shipped to different places to different work, and they did  not survive. Just lucky. Just lucky to be in the right time, to know some people. We worked together. And we worked together to help-- to help each other too, you know. If somebody get another piece of bread, or something, he'd share with you. That's what we-- that's how we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember that, people sharing and helping each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, of course. Of course. A lot of people shared their soup. If they got something, bread or soup, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that unusual, considering that people were starving?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not unusual with our group. It depends what your environment, what your group were. You didn't-- you didn't share with everybody. You shared with your-- with your group you were in, because you couldn't share with everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you-- you, you left Plaszow in what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: '45. And where did you go then?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945, we marched out in, in January the 14th. I think the 14th. We marched out at night to, to Auschwitz, to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a death march?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just a march. We were-- we were-- we were still in, in, in very good health, you know, comparing, comparing to the-- to the future camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it wasn't that far from Auschwitz, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we went-- we went overnight. And then we slept over on the way in, in in [INAUDIBLE] or some place. And then we came to, to Birkenau the next day. It was not-- it was not too, too bad, you know. We were still-- everybody was still healthy. And we came to Birkenau. And in Birkenau we stayed, I think, a couple or three days. I'm not sure. Two days. And then we went by foot then, again, to Gleiwitz. That was the death march really. That was the beginning of the death march. There was already snow, full of snowfall. Then we had to walk up to Gleiwitz. And lots of people already couldn't make it, not maybe from our group, from some other groups, because we already-- a larger transport was not on our transport. There was a lot of people going from, from Birkenau. And a lot of people couldn't make it. They were already shot on the-- on the road to Gleiwitz, right on the-- right on the spot in the snow. And we came to Gleiwitz. We stayed overnight in Gleiwitz in the barrack. Then they, they load us on the-- on the open railroad cars. And we were-- we were traveling for about a week through-- I don't know-- through Czechoslovakia toward Germany. We were traveling for seven, eight days without food, without--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a destination or just traveling?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. We were just traveling, I guess. They, they told us later on-- I don't know-- there were rumors that some camps wouldn't accept us. They were over filled or something. Mauthausen wouldn't accept us or some places, Gross-Rosen. I'm not sure. But the destination we ended up in Sachsenhausen, which is outside of Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for a week you were without food in the cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right. Once in a while, they threw some bread up. We were-- we were-- I don't know how many, 80 or 100 people on there. I'm not sure. So they threw up maybe 10, 10 loaves of bread. So who, who grabbed the bread had a piece of bread. Who didn't-- who didn't grab a bread or who didn't get a piece of bread didn't get any bread. But we traveled through Czechoslovakia. And some, some place in Czechoslovakia, we stopped at some railroad crossings. And people going, going through, through-- they saw us in the-- in the trucks. And they threw some food out at us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the cattle cars were open?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. They were open cattle cars. So a lot of people, you know, jumped from the cattle cars and tried to run away. They, they were-- got killed right on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people freeze in those cattle cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Freeze in the cattle cars. Some of them just-- as a matter of fact, I, I remember a friend of mine, he start to take  his clothes off. He must have got-- he was getting-- was getting sick. He was getting a fever. He was taking his clothes off. He took his clothes off, and he, he froze to death right on the-- on the-- on the-- on the car-- on the truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a lot of dead people arrived at, at, at Sachsenhausen. At Sachsenhausen, we were unloaded. We stayed over there-- I don't know-- a day or two. And they-- we were shipped to Flossenburg. In Flossenburg, we were then in the quarantine. They, they, they cleaned us up again, gave us some, some, some-- what are they called? The striped, striped suits. And we were shipped to a place in either-- in Bavaria named Ganacker. Do you remember how to spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. G-A-N-A-C-K-E-R. It's a small village. Ganacker is close to-- not too far from Regensburg. And over there, we're working on the-- on the airfield. We came to the place. It was-- it was nothing there. It was just a big hanger, hanger. We all had to sleep on the floor. And we had to build a little  kitchen on the site. We were-- we were not getting any food for a while, just bread. And then after they built a kitchen, we had-- we had to go to work. Five some. We were not too many over there, maybe 60 people or so, not too many. And then coming from work, we used to get every five men, staying in, in the particular row, used to get a washbasin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they walked up to the-- to the-- to the kitchen. And the wash-- they, they give you soup at the washbasin. And everybody had a spoon. And everybody had to eat from the washbasin. You had to be lucky to be with somebody couldn't eat fast enough or he knows you. If not, you would be left with just water on top. That's how they'd feed us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, when you arrived in Birkenau, did you see the chimneys? Did you see the smoke?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were no more chimneys when we arrived in Birkenau. We didn't-- we didn't-- we, we didn't know about it. We didn't see it anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't see it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see it. Maybe there were, but I didn't see any chimneys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we didn't see anything. We were only over there two days, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. What do you remember about the other camps you were in? Do you remember anything you can tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the last camp, in Ganacker, in, in April, 19-- in 1945, we came to-- we came-- we, we're working, like I told you, at the airfield. And they make a big Appelplatz. On the Appelplatz, you know, they called all the prisoners out. And they, they told us to get ready. We're going to march out. We knew it was something coming, because the airfield we were working on was striped by American planes. And we were so happy to see it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The German guards, they were hiding. They were telling-- they were afraid. We  were not hiding. We, we, we, we didn't-- we, we didn't want to hide. We were just looking up. They come to stripe the airfield. And they were shooting at the German planes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- [SOBS] it was quite a sight, you know, to see. But anyway-- I'm getting-- I'm getting emotional. I remember. But anyway, the 25th of April, we were supposed to march out from this particular camp. We knew it was already the end of the war almost. We didn't know how long it will last.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took out 10 people from the camp again, some young, young guys, to bring some bread from the-- from the work, or from the kitchen. And we came. And I was between them. So I went to the kitchen to see a whole bread. It was like a dream. It was like a dream to see a bread. So I-- so I took a bread, and I put it under my coat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And me, stupid, I was quite, you know, skinny. They sticked out. The bread sticked out. The minute I came back with the bread, they saw that I-- that I had the bread on me. They pulled me out, and they took-- and they took-- they took me on the-- on the Appelplatz in the-- in the center. And they start to beat me. They beat me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, why did you took the bread? Why did you take the bread? I said, I was hungry. I needed the bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all the people around me, all my friends, were around the Appelplatz, you know, you know, standing around. And they were just beating and beating and beating me-- and I don't know how long it took-- with, with, with sticks and, and, and steel bars. Then they say they're going to hang me. You're going to be hanged.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at the last minute, I look at the people, and I said, my god. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mind went through, I survived so long. And this is the last minute. What did I do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said-- I said to the people, Polish. [POLISH], just like that. The minute they heard that, they said, you-- what did you talk to the-- there were some Russian people with us too. He said in German, [GERMAN]. You know, they were afraid that I spoke to the Russians something against them. So they, they, they pulled me, and they're going to hang me.
